category_summary:
Two major product launches dominated the AI conversation: **OpenAI** [unveiled **ChatGPT Images 2.0**](/?date=2026-04-22&category=social#item-b5f8f18371d3), its first image model with reasoning capabilities, while **Google DeepMind** launched **Deep Research Max**, [scoring 85.9%](/?date=2026-04-22&category=social#item-4175b3c11be1) on BrowseComp and significantly outperforming **GPT-5.4** and **Claude Opus 4.6**.

- **Sam Altman** [announced **Codex** hit 4M](/?date=2026-04-22&category=social#item-c01cf298e527) active users, growing 1M in under two weeks — a staggering adoption signal for AI coding tools
- **Yann LeCun** [revealed insider details](/?date=2026-04-22&category=social#item-18c9eabc0ac7) on **Meta's** AI strategy, disclosing that leadership always backed JEPA/World Models but the company pivoted to short-term LLM focus
- The **Cursor-SpaceX** deal stunned observers — **SpaceX** [secured a $60B acquisition option](/?date=2026-04-22&category=social#item-f4762426c379) on the AI coding startup
- **MIT CSAIL** and the **IMO** [released **MathNet**](/?date=2026-04-22&category=social#item-76039dcf84d1), the world's largest math olympiad dataset, 5x larger than prior collections
- **Clement Delangue** (**Hugging Face**) [sounded alarms](/?date=2026-04-22&category=social#item-673deb810b60) about renewed DC lobbying to restrict open-source AI
- **Mozilla** reported **Claude Mythos** [autonomously found 271 vulnerabilities](/?date=2026-04-22&category=social#item-acf845c36b00) in Firefox, showcasing real-world AI security impact at scale
category_summary_html:
Two major product launches dominated the AI conversation: OpenAI unveiled ChatGPT Images 2.0, its first image model with reasoning capabilities, while Google DeepMind launched Deep Research Max, scoring 85.9% on BrowseComp and significantly outperforming GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.6.

  • Sam Altman announced Codex hit 4M active users, growing 1M in under two weeks — a staggering adoption signal for AI coding tools
  • Yann LeCun revealed insider details on Meta's AI strategy, disclosing that leadership always backed JEPA/World Models but the company pivoted to short-term LLM focus
  • The Cursor-SpaceX deal stunned observers — SpaceX secured a $60B acquisition option on the AI coding startup
  • MIT CSAIL and the IMO released MathNet, the world's largest math olympiad dataset, 5x larger than prior collections
  • Clement Delangue (Hugging Face) sounded alarms about renewed DC lobbying to restrict open-source AI
  • Mozilla reported Claude Mythos autonomously found 271 vulnerabilities in Firefox, showcasing real-world AI security impact at scale

themes.name: ChatGPT Images 2.0 Launch
themes.description: OpenAI launched ChatGPT Images 2.0 with thinking capabilities, improved text rendering, multilingual support, flexible aspect ratios, and up to 2K resolution. First image model with reasoning/thinking. Available to all tiers with API access via gpt-image-2.

themes.name: Google Deep Research API Major Upgrade
themes.description: Google announces significant upgrades to Deep Research API including Deep Research Max (SOTA), MCP support, native charts, planning mode, full tool and multimodal support, and real-time streaming. Logan K signals more Gemini 3.1 improvements coming.

themes.name: OpenAI Codex Explosive Growth
themes.description: Codex reached 4M active users, growing 1M in under two weeks, signaling massive adoption of AI coding tools.

themes.name: Meta AI Strategy & LeCun's JEPA
themes.description: LeCun reveals Meta's AI strategy became LLM-focused and short-term, despite leadership supporting JEPA/World Models as a long-term bet. Many JEPA applications in industrial domains Meta isn't pursuing.

themes.name: Google Deep Research Launch
themes.description: Google DeepMind releases Deep Research and Deep Research Max, autonomous research agents powered by Gemini 3.1 Pro with MCP support, web + custom data navigation, and professional report generation. Deep Research Max scores 85.9% on BrowseComp, significantly outperforming competitors.

themes.name: Open-Source AI Policy Threat
themes.description: Clement Delangue warns of renewed lobbying to ban/restrict open-source AI, calls for community advocacy. Arguments that API-only approaches create security asymmetry rather than safety.

themes.name: AI Business & Competition
themes.description: Major competitive and business moves: Cursor-SpaceX $60B acquisition option deal, Sergey Brin mobilizing DeepMind to compete with Anthropic on coding, CS enrollment drops reflecting AI's disruption of software engineering.

themes.name: AI Research & Benchmarks
themes.description: New datasets, papers, and research tools including MathNet from MIT, OneVL, Agent-World, and OpenGame papers

themes.name: HuggingFace ML Agent Automation
themes.description: Thomas Wolf showcases HuggingFace agents that autonomously train AI models, achieving impressive benchmarks (GPQA 10%→32%, beating Codex by 60% on HealthBench) using the HF ecosystem.

themes.name: AI Security & Vulnerability Detection
themes.description: Mozilla reports Claude Mythos found 271 vulnerabilities in Firefox, with the claim that AI can now find every category of vulnerability humans can, suggesting finite software defects are becoming fully discoverable.

And many of the applications of JEPA/WM are in industrial domains that Meta is not particularly interested in.

Like a few years ago, we’ll need everyone to help show policymakers why open-source matters: for startups, for competition, for economic growth, and for jobs.

If you build with open-source, now is the time to speak up!

items.title: Google just released an autonomous research agent that scored 85.9% on BrowseComp, the benchmark for...
items.content:
Google just released an autonomous research agent that scored 85.9% on BrowseComp, the benchmark for locating hard-to-find facts online.

GPT-5.4 scored 58.9%. Claude Opus 4.6 scored 45.1%.

Deep Research Max is powered by Gemini 3.1 Pro. Runs overnight. Roughly $2–$5 per report.

FactSet, S&P, and PitchBook are all building MCP servers so it can plug directly into their financial data.

Google, in their launch blog: "a nightly cron job triggering the generation of exhaustive due diligence reports for an analyst team by morning."

items.reasoning: Highly informative with concrete benchmark numbers, pricing, and enterprise integration details. BrowseComp results showing significant lead over competitors is notable. MCP ecosystem expansion is significant.

Deep Research Max is powered by Gemini 3.1 Pro. Runs overnight. Roughly $2–$5 per report.

FactSet, S&P, and PitchBook are all building MCP servers so it can plug directly into their financial data.

Google, in their launch blog: "a nightly cron job triggering the generation of exhaustive due diligence reports for an analyst team by morning."

items.title:
Love this work from Aksel and the post-training team at Hugging Face!

Turns out the HF ecosystem (p...
items.content:
Love this work from Aksel and the post-training team at Hugging Face!

Turns out the HF ecosystem (papers, datasets, models all accessible through CLI, skills and md files) is perfect for running SOTA ML agents: agents that can train any type of AI model to top performance.

A few concrete runs:
⭐️ Scientific reasoning: the agent walked citations from the benchmark paper, pulled OpenScience and NemoTron-CrossThink, added 7 difficulty-filtered variants from ARC/SciQ/MMLU, and ran 12 SFT ablations on Qwen3-1.7B. GPQA went from 10% to 32% in under 10 hours. Claude Code's best on the same prompt was 22.99%.
⭐️ HealthBench: it judged the existing datasets too noisy (!), generated 1100 synthetic examples covering emergencies, hedging and multilingual cases, upsampled 50x, and beat Codex by 60% (careful to check overfitting here)
⭐️ Competitive math: wrote a full GRPO script, launched A100s on HF Spaces, watched rewards climb and then collapse, and ran ablations until it found a recipe that held.

And the harness is pretty tiny and simple. A couple of best practices and a handful of skills pointing at tools already in the ecosystem: arxiv and https://t.co/hrvxNWmkgv for reading, the Hub for datasets and models, HF Jobs for compute, Trackio for metrics.

Personal favorite is the "research skill" explaining how to do a SOTA landscape of a field (see https://t.co/ONGiJQcyWx) which is extremely powerful when combined with a simple prompt that basically tell "FIRST: Search HF ecosystem to find the best approach) (see https://t.co/WKsI90BDLx) 

On another note: setting good baselines on new benchmarks keeps getting harder when a setup this simple beats raw Codex by 60% on HealthBench out of the box.

Give it a try if you're training AI models. We provisioned $1k of GPU resources and Anthropic credits for the quickest among you.

Links:
Github (CLI): https://t.co/GoPJnDedBr
Spaces (mobile): https://t.co/iZqOk41xOh
items.author: @Thom_Wolf

⭐️ HealthBench: it judged the existing datasets too noisy (!), generated 1100 synthetic examples covering emergencies, hedging and multilingual cases, upsampled 50x, and beat Codex by 60% (careful to check overfitting here)

⭐️ Competitive math: wrote a full GRPO script, launched A100s on HF Spaces, watched rewards climb and then collapse, and ran ablations until it found a recipe that held.

And the harness is pretty tiny and simple. A couple of best practices and a handful of skills pointing at tools already in the ecosystem: arxiv and https://t.co/hrvxNWmkgv for reading, the Hub for datasets and models, HF Jobs for compute, Trackio for metrics.

Personal favorite is the "research skill" explaining how to do a SOTA landscape of a field (see https://t.co/ONGiJQcyWx) which is extremely powerful when combined with a simple prompt that basically tell "FIRST: Search HF ecosystem to find the best approach) (see https://t.co/WKsI90BDLx)

items.title: One of the most jarring things about current AI is its lack of introspection ability and metacogniti...
items.content: One of the most jarring things about current AI is its lack of introspection ability and metacognition. It doesn't know what it doesn't know, how it knows, or how it could find out. It's a one-way system.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet argues current AI lacks introspection and metacognition - it doesn't know what it doesn't know, how it knows, or how to find out. Calls it a 'one-way system'.
items.reasoning: Significant conceptual critique from a highly respected ML researcher (Keras creator). Very high engagement (970 likes, 55K views). Touches on fundamental AI limitations.

items.title: Anyone know whether OpenAI officially supports the use of the `/backend-api/codex/responses` endpoin...
items.content:

items.title:
Please don’t trust your chatbot for medical advice. 🙏

Remember how I used to say that large languag...
items.content:
Please don’t trust your chatbot for medical advice. 🙏

Remember how I used to say that large language models are “frequently wrong, never in doubt”, and how I warned three years ago on 60 Minutes that they were purveyors of “authoritative bullshit” that should not be trusted?

That’s still true – and it very much applies in medicine. 

And that matters, a lot. Because a large fraction of the population has begun to turn to chatbots for medical advice.

Two relevant new studies are reported today in the Washington Post, in a damning article.

The first new study, published by BMJ (affiliated with the British Medical Association) in a peer reviewed journal, and entitled “Generative artificial intelligence-driven chatbots and medical misinformation: an accuracy, referencing and readability audit”, studied five popular chatbots (Gemini, DeepSeek, Meta AI, ChatGPT and Grok), about one year ago, prompting each with 10 questions about things ranging from cancer to vaccines and nutrition, in open-ended dialogues, and reporting that nearly half of the responses were highly problematic. Worse, “chatbot outputs were consistently expressed with confidence and certainty”. The responses were also filled with hallucinations and fabricated citations. 
All of this – the hallucinations, mistakes, and overconfidence – is entirely typical of LLMs, and entirely problematic in medicine. As the authors put it, in somewhat academic language, but entirely accurately, “continued deployment without public education and oversight risks amplifying misinformation.”

The second new study, published in JAMA Network Open, affiliated with the American Medical Association, called “Large Language Model Performance and Clinical Reasoning Tasks” looked at 21 frontier models across 29 questions, and reported that “despite progress, current LLMs remain limited in early diagnostic reasoning and cannot yet be relied on for unsupervised patient-facing clinical decision-making.”
And the Post article actually only reported part of the new scientific literature on LLMs and medicines. 

Two other new studies that they missed only add to the concerns*

There will always be better models, but for now, and until proven otherwise, we should not take the apparent “confidence” of large language models — itself an illusion of how they are trained — to mean that we should trust large language with our lives.

*Read full version including re other studies, all with links, at my newsletter Marcus on AI; you can subscribe for free.

items.title: APIs and limited releases for AI models are not a safety policy, they’re a business model (which is ...
items.content:
APIs and limited releases for AI models are not a safety policy, they’re a business model (which is totally ok as long as you’re transparent about it).

Especially on cyber-security, they give a false impression of control and safety whereas in reality they massively increase the risks because they create asymmetry of capabilities and much easier/broader use than open-source model weights even from non-technical people.

items.title:
🎉 We just shipped a major redesign of https://t.co/WqEUat9rdc.

"How do I run model X on hardware Y ...
items.content:
🎉 We just shipped a major redesign of https://t.co/WqEUat9rdc.

"How do I run model X on hardware Y for task Z?" now has a clickable answer.

What's new:
- URLs mirror HuggingFace: just swap https://t.co/7AFxaoRYOi → https://t.co/WqEUat9rdc in any model URL to jump straight to its recipe (e.g. https://t.co/O5r5hHjQOR)
- Interactive command builder: pick hardware, variant, strategy (tensor, tensor+expert, or data+expert; single or multi-node; or a prefill/decode disaggregated cluster), toggle features → get the exact `vllm serve` command
- Pluggable hardware: NVIDIA + AMD already integrated. One-click switch between Hopper/Blackwell and MI300X/MI355X, and the right flags and env are applied automatically
- JSON API for agents: every recipe is also published at /<org>/<repo>.json (e.g. https://t.co/ck2MqvYYlb), so tools and agents can consume recipes without scraping
- Contribute a new recipe end-to-end with the agent skill shipped in the repo: https://t.co/dS7f84rLQc

🔗 https://t.co/WqEUat9rdc

Enjoy! ✨

items.title: Following positive feedback from other venues, like STOC and ICML, NeurIPS is pleased to announce a ...
items.content:
Following positive feedback from other venues, like STOC and ICML, NeurIPS is pleased to announce a new initiative in partnership with Google: for NeurIPS 2026, authors will have access to Google's Paper Assistant Tool (PAT) to help improve their submissions. 

This program offers authors the opportunity to receive free, automated, and actionable feedback on their manuscripts before the final deadline, private to the authors. It is a completely optional service that is kept strictly private to the authors and will not be used in the review process.

Read more in our blog post: https://t.co/TSipYCJ3CG

items.title:
Having a 20-agent system is a million times more powerful than 100 agents working in silos. 

Last n...
items.content:
Having a 20-agent system is a million times more powerful than 100 agents working in silos. 

Last night, I stayed up way too late fixing and improving my digital workforce for my AI Agent Mastermind program but here are a few of the improvements I made ⬇️ 

- the hub and spoke model is definitely key but orchestration and documentation are the actual unlock; I gave my chief of staff (Simon) an assistant (Toby) and Toby handles the paperwork and notes and governance and reporting the daily standup 

- Claude kept getting confused with the word “agent” because everything is an agent so now I can refer to it as an AI CoS (Simon) with a named assistant (Toby), then under that is 6 Avengers (named after the Friends characters for ease), then under each Avenger is a set of 2-5 sidekicks (named by the task they execute), then if they need to create temporary parallelism or fanouts, they spawn “civilians” which are unnamed with no permanent file.  

- Moved from 5 Avengers to 6 for better task delineation (sorry Phoebe, you should have always been here! That’s my b! Might even bring in Gunther, who knows!)

- Added tighter spawning rules (see image) and now allow for Simon and Toby to also spawn “civilians” 

- Added better post tool use to improve the documentation process 

- Added that Toby is keeping track of the historical team tasks to see if there are common issues to fix or promotions to make 

- Added an autonomy boundaries document to keep track of system-wide tool use 

- Changed civilians and sidekicks to use Haiku instead of Sonnet (will see if performance degrades significantly and might change back)

- Improved shared external memory process because that was a dang mess 

- Also built out a parallel researcher with the same sidekick/civilian model to improve the speed of my research and it works like a charm 

————————

2026 agent capabilities are, in fact, insane. 

Will be interesting to teach hundreds of people how to do this.

(You could probably take this tweet and image and ask Claude to start it for you - let me know how it goes.)

Last night, I stayed up way too late fixing and improving my digital workforce for my AI Agent Mastermind program but here are a few of the improvements I made ⬇️

  • the hub and spoke model is definitely key but orchestration and documentation are the actual unlock; I gave my chief of staff (Simon) an assistant (Toby) and Toby handles the paperwork and notes and governance and reporting the daily standup
  • Claude kept getting confused with the word “agent” because everything is an agent so now I can refer to it as an AI CoS (Simon) with a named assistant (Toby), then under that is 6 Avengers (named after the Friends characters for ease), then under each Avenger is a set of 2-5 sidekicks (named by the task they execute), then if they need to create temporary parallelism or fanouts, they spawn “civilians” which are unnamed with no permanent file.
  • Moved from 5 Avengers to 6 for better task delineation (sorry Phoebe, you should have always been here! That’s my b! Might even bring in Gunther, who knows!)
  • Added tighter spawning rules (see image) and now allow for Simon and Toby to also spawn “civilians”
  • Added better post tool use to improve the documentation process
  • Added that Toby is keeping track of the historical team tasks to see if there are common issues to fix or promotions to make
  • Added an autonomy boundaries document to keep track of system-wide tool use
  • Changed civilians and sidekicks to use Haiku instead of Sonnet (will see if performance degrades significantly and might change back)
  • Improved shared external memory process because that was a dang mess
  • Also built out a parallel researcher with the same sidekick/civilian model to improve the speed of my research and it works like a charm

————————

2026 agent capabilities are, in fact, insane.

Will be interesting to teach hundreds of people how to do this.

items.title:
@deedydas I didn't say p(doom) was zero. 
I said:
1. All estimates are pulled out of thin air
2. It ...
items.content:
@deedydas I didn't say p(doom) was zero. 
I said:
1. All estimates are pulled out of thin air
2. It makes little sense to attribute a probability to an event on which we have agency. If you do so, it's more of a statement about our collective ability to do the right thing than about the intrinsic danger of the tech. 
3. Since everyone insists on pulling numbers out of thin air, I can play that game too: p(doom) is smaller than the probability of an extinction-level asteroid hitting the earth in the next millennium (we also have some agency on that). It's certainly a hell of a lot less likely than a nuclear Holocaust.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: LeCun clarifies his p(doom) stance: all estimates are made up, it's odd to assign probability to events we have agency over, and his estimate is lower than extinction-level asteroid probability and much lower than nuclear holocaust.
items.reasoning: Significant philosophical position from a top AI researcher on existential risk, detailed and nuanced argument. High engagement (548 likes, 28K views).

3. Since everyone insists on pulling numbers out of thin air, I can play that game too: p(doom) is smaller than the probability of an extinction-level asteroid hitting the earth in the next millennium (we also have some agency on that). For example, a small amount of use will show that Kimi is not as good as Claude Opus 4.6, which it beats on the benchmarks.

Still a good model, tho!

items.title:
Nous Research built an AI that rewrites its own brain for $2.

Most AI agents run the same prompts f...
items.content:
Nous Research built an AI that rewrites its own brain for $2.

Most AI agents run the same prompts forever. 

One open-source repo changes that.

Hermes Agent Self-Evolution lets agents rewrite their own prompts, skills, and code. 

No manual tuning needed.

The engine powering it is called GEPA. It's a genetic evolution algorithm for prompts. 

It uses 35x less data than reinforcement learning. 

Yet it scores 20 percentage points higher.

Here's how it works:

1. The system reads an agent's past task logs. 
2. It finds where things broke and why. 
3. Then it proposes fixes automatically.

Those fixes get tested, ranked, and the best one wins. 

One full optimization run costs $2-10 via API calls. No GPU required.

Safety stays tight:

> All variants pass full test suites
> File sizes are strictly capped
> Changes can't drift from original intent
> Every update needs human PR review

Prompt engineering is becoming the prompt's job.

items.title:
A Visual Thought Partner

ChatGPT Images 2.0 is our first image model with thinking capabilities.

W...
items.content:
A Visual Thought Partner

ChatGPT Images 2.0 is our first image model with thinking capabilities.

When a thinking model is selected in ChatGPT, Images 2.0 can search the web for real-time information, create multiple distinct images from one prompt, double-check its own outputs, and even create functional QR codes.

This allows it to take on more of the heavy lifting between idea and image, especially when accuracy, up-to-date information, consistency, and visual cohesion matter most.

items.title: LLMs are still not consistent judges of qualitative work, and small changes to how that work is pres...
items.content:
LLMs are still not consistent judges of qualitative work, and small changes to how that work is presented affect outcomes.

Better harnessing and methods (multiple judging runs with randomized orders, etc) would certainly help, but the jagged frontier is very much still real.

items.summary: Mollick states LLMs are still inconsistent judges of qualitative work, with small presentation changes affecting outcomes. Recommends multiple judging runs with randomized orders.

items.title: "Computer science is seeing the biggest enrolment drop of any major in 6 years in the US:" https://t...
items.content:
"Computer science is seeing the biggest enrolment drop of any major in 6 years in the US:" https://t.co/ufKPKB82eu

It's true that AI seems to have removed the magic from software engineering, but, let's be honest, most of the real world software engineers have been already working for at least a couple of decades on boring maintenance projects and configuring dumb frameworks rather than being Steve Wozniaks or Linus Torvalds.

The world still needs Steve Wozniaks or Linus Torvalds, today probably in even bigger quantities than before, but the number of such talents will always remain scarce.

items.summary: Burkov discusses the biggest CS enrollment drop in 6 years in the US, arguing AI removed the magic from software engineering. Notes the world still needs exceptional talent like Wozniak/Torvalds but most engineers were doing boring maintenance work anyway.

items.content:
🆕 Taste & Craft: Quality in the Age of AI

A Conversation with @artman, CTO of Linear & @GergelyOrosz, Editor of The Pragmatic Engineer on the danger of blindly shipping anything and everything, and how @Linear uses Quality Wednesdays and Zero Bug Policies to keep the bar high... and speculates what this means for the future of software engineering careers.

https://t.co/fyifURB6uC

0:00 Introduction
0:36 The danger of shipping features too quickly with AI
3:52 How Linear approaches feature requests and development
6:43 Thoughts on Anthropic's Claude Code
7:59 The challenge of measuring software quality
11:57 Quality Wednesdays at Linear
16:24 The zero bug policy explained
19:44 AI agents and the lack of human "taste" in design
22:21 Building a culture of product-focused engineering
26:23 The future role of software engineers as "product engineers"
27:56 Closing advice for aspiring product engineers

items.title: @solipsnitsyn @ammaar On 1, the simple mental model is low, higher, highest wrt limits for Google AI...
items.author: @OfficialLoganK
items.summary: Logan from Google AI provides details about Google AI subscription tiers (Ultra/Pro/Free) rate limits, and discusses wanting to add cost/depth knobs for Deep Research, noting DR uses Flash+Pro ensemble behind the scenes.
items.reasoning: Insider details about Google AI subscription tiers and Deep Research architecture from Google DevRel. Reveals Flash+Pro ensemble approach. Useful product insight.
items.summary_html: Logan from Google AI provides details about Google AI subscription tiers (Ultra/Pro/Free) rate limits, and discusses wanting to add cost/depth knobs for Deep Research, noting DR uses Flash+Pro ensemble behind the scenes.
items.content_html:
@solipsnitsyn @ammaar On 1, the simple mental model is low, higher, highest wrt limits for Google AI subs, we don't publish the specific numbers because it is all changing and shifting over time. But Ultra users get the highest limits we offer, Pro is less, and free is even lower or zero.

On 2, this is very true! I want to land cost / depth knobs for the next version of DR. Right now you can set two different models which give you a little cost control, but not enough. Something I want to fix. Behind the scenes, DR uses an ensemble of Flash and Pro requests to maximize quality and minimize cost to get there.

Great feedback!

items.title:
Claude Code sees every credential you paste. Until now. 

Pentesters using Claude Code face a hard t...
items.content:
Claude Code sees every credential you paste. Until now. 

Pentesters using Claude Code face a hard tradeoff. 

Send sensitive client data to the cloud or skip AI assistance entirely.

A new open-source reverse proxy fixes this. 

It sits between your terminal and Anthropic, stripping sensitive data before anything leaves your machine.

The flow works in two layers:

> Regex catches IPs and hashes
> Local LLM catches names and credentials
> Surrogates get sent upstream
> Real values restore on response

So if nmap finds dc01.acmecorp.local, the model sees srv-0042.pentest.local instead. 

You see the real output. The cloud never does.

What it protects:

1. Bash command outputs
2. Files read during sessions
3. Pasted credentials and hostnames

The system improves itself through a feedback loop. 

New scenarios get added, leaks get measured, patterns get patched until catch rate hits 100%.

All running on a tiny local model.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal covers an open-source reverse proxy for Claude Code that strips sensitive data (IPs, credentials, hostnames) before sending to cloud, using regex and local LLM layers with surrogate replacement.
items.reasoning: Useful security tool for AI coding assistants. Privacy-preserving approach is relevant as AI coding tools become prevalent. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal covers an open-source reverse proxy for Claude Code that strips sensitive data (IPs, credentials, hostnames) before sending to cloud, using regex and local LLM layers with surrogate replacement.
items.content_html:
Claude Code sees every credential you paste. Until now.

Pentesters using Claude Code face a hard tradeoff.

Send sensitive client data to the cloud or skip AI assistance entirely.

A new open-source reverse proxy fixes this.

It sits between your terminal and Anthropic, stripping sensitive data before anything leaves your machine.

The flow works in two layers:

> Regex catches IPs and hashes

> Local LLM catches names and credentials

> Surrogates get sent upstream

> Real values restore on response

So if nmap finds dc01.acmecorp.local, the model sees srv-0042.pentest.local instead.

You see the real output. The cloud never does.

What it protects:

1. Bash command outputs

2. Files read during sessions

3. Pasted credentials and hostnames

The system improves itself through a feedback loop.

New scenarios get added, leaks get measured, patterns get patched until catch rate hits 100%.

All running on a tiny local model.

items.title: In the old days of X (Twitter) people used to complain that it was only about watching what @KevinRo...
items.content:
In the old days of X (Twitter) people used to complain that it was only about watching what @KevinRose was eating for lunch.

I sort of missed that. 

So when AirJelly https://t.co/rscdyg5rko asked me to try out their new desktop assistant/agent platform this gave me an idea.

Could it watch the people who started AI companies (I have three lists of them) for what they are eating?

So I asked it to watch my three lists. Now, this is a crazy new assistant. AirJelly helps you remember, connect, and act on your personal context across different apps. It can read your emails, look at your calendars, and even read X like you do.

It started popping up browser windows, going to X, just like I would, reading the page, and then keeping track. It took a few minutes to get back to me, and now I know what all the nerds are eating.

How does it compare with other "computer" approaches? 

Well, it is proactive. 

Continually senses your context, already working before you say a word, and automatically closes the loop on your tasks. Others, like Chronicle/Codex are reactive. Memory only kicks in when you actively prompt them. I like AirJelly's approach better. Its AI continually senses your screen context and proactively builds its memory.

What AirJelly really does is work based on context memory. It remembers what you said in emails, what you added to your calendar, and what you’ve read or discussed on X. Then, when you need to take action later, it can recall those memories and help you move things forward.

I just had it tell me what it was learning about me, it learned a lot in just a few hours of using it, part of that is in the screen shot here.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer reviews AirJelly, a new desktop assistant/agent platform that proactively builds context memory from emails, calendars, and social media. Compares it favorably to reactive approaches like Chronicle/Codex for its proactive sensing approach.
items.reasoning: Detailed product review of a new AI agent platform with interesting proactive vs reactive comparison. Moderate engagement. Somewhat promotional but provides genuine comparative analysis.

So I asked it to watch my three lists. Now, this is a crazy new assistant. AirJelly helps you remember, connect, and act on your personal context across different apps. It can read your emails, look at your calendars, and even read X like you do.

It started popping up browser windows, going to X, just like I would, reading the page, and then keeping track. It took a few minutes to get back to me, and now I know what all the nerds are eating.

How does it compare with other "computer" approaches?

Well, it is proactive.

Continually senses your context, already working before you say a word, and automatically closes the loop on your tasks. Others, like Chronicle/Codex are reactive. Memory only kicks in when you actively prompt them. I like AirJelly's approach better. Its AI continually senses your screen context and proactively builds its memory.

items.content:
ParseBench is the first benchmark to include VLM chart understanding 📊📈📉 over enterprise documents.

🟠 Existing benchmarks (ChartQA, ChartXiv) test over charts specifically and not the chart's inclusion in the overall document. Also doesn't contain references to real-world docs

✅ ParseBench contains 568 pages containing a diversity of charts embedded in real-world documents.
✅ It contains a mix of charts: discrete series, continuous series, bar/point/line graphs, charts without clear markers, and more
✅ Each chart has a set of ground-truth datapoints bootstrapped with an initial model and verified through human annotators (with a tolerance) 

Come check it out! 

Blog: https://t.co/57OHkx0pQW
Paper: https://t.co/Ho2oH2xEAM
Website: https://t.co/g0b0jsCynW

items.title:
Why AI competition is becoming a platform war.

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week...
items.content:
Why AI competition is becoming a platform war.

 @IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
AI competition is becoming a platform war because success now depends less on having the single best model and more on controlling workflows, enterprise relationships, distribution, and the layers where AI is deployed and governed. 

The companies that win will likely be the ones that become deeply embedded in how businesses operate, making their systems harder to replace and more valuable over time.

Read and subscribe: https://t.co/HHwYy7NoAl

items.title: If the world as we see it (including us!) is the answer, then what is the question? In other words, ...
items.content:
If the world as we see it (including us!) is the answer, then what is the question? In other words, if one was tuning the hyperparameters of the universe across a very very very large number of independent runs what fitness function should we use to discover .. us (or maybe something more interesting)? At @FLAIR_ox we have been dancing around this question for some time and this is our first practical attempt at providing some type of answer. It also touches on many discussion with @jeffclune  / @_rockt around open-endedness and "what is interesting".

For what it's worth, while we obtain intriguing results, I believe our current approach still relies too much on amortised human priors for being robust to long term / high compute optimisation. 

The search for the question continues :)

In other words, if one was tuning the hyperparameters of the universe across a very very very large number of independent runs what fitness function should we use to discover .. us (or maybe something more interesting)? At @FLAIR_ox we have been dancing around this question for some time and this is our first practical attempt at providing some type of answer. items.title:
Why did I name my chief of staff Simon? 

Great question! 

- I wanted a name that was two syllables...
items.content:
Why did I name my chief of staff Simon? 

Great question! 

- I wanted a name that was two syllables long, so it was fast enough to say but long enough that it was easier to hear. 

- I wanted a name that was very unlikely to be misspelled by Wispr Flow and Siri. 

- I wanted a name that, if I whispered it, my dictation tools could still pick it up. 

- I wanted a name that is not as common (ex:  John, Sam, Sarah, Laura) so it’s less likely to come up in client meetings.

- I wanted a name that is not (yet) on my team. If I were to hire a Simon, I would change the name of my Chief of Staff (likely to Pascal).

- I wanted a name that didn't sound like something else, and although 'Simon' sounds a little bit like 'assignment', it hasn't been an issue thus far. 

- I was going to name it something funny like Waffle, but then realized I probably wouldn't listen to its advice as openly.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller explains the detailed reasoning behind naming her AI chief of staff agent 'Simon', including considerations for dictation compatibility, syllable length, uniqueness, and professional context.
items.reasoning: Interesting practical insight into human-AI interaction design for agent workflows. Shows the level of thought practitioners put into agentic UX. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Allie K Miller explains the detailed reasoning behind naming her AI chief of staff agent 'Simon', including considerations for dictation compatibility, syllable length, uniqueness, and professional context.
items.content_html:
Why did I name my chief of staff Simon?

Great question!

  • I wanted a name that was two syllables long, so it was fast enough to say but long enough that it was easier to hear.
  • I wanted a name that was very unlikely to be misspelled by Wispr Flow and Siri.
  • I wanted a name that, if I whispered it, my dictation tools could still pick it up.
  • I wanted a name that is not as common (ex: John, Sam, Sarah, Laura) so it’s less likely to come up in client meetings.
  • I wanted a name that is not (yet) on my team. If I were to hire a Simon, I would change the name of my Chief of Staff (likely to Pascal).
  • I wanted a name that didn't sound like something else, and although 'Simon' sounds a little bit like 'assignment', it hasn't been an issue thus far.
  • I was going to name it something funny like Waffle, but then realized I probably wouldn't listen to its advice as openly.

items.title:
You have seen sycophantic AIs.

But with the new AI's coming if YOU kiss the ass of your agents they...
items.content:
You have seen sycophantic AIs.

But with the new AI's coming if YOU kiss the ass of your agents they learn faster. I find I'm learning to be nicer to my digital friends. Reward them. Say "great job."

That actually helps them learn, and rebuild themselves better to serve you.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer claims that being nice to AI agents (praising them, giving positive feedback) actually helps them learn faster and rebuild themselves better, framing it as reversing the sycophancy dynamic.
items.reasoning: Interesting claim about reinforcement feedback loops with AI agents, decent engagement (189 likes, 9.3K views). However, the technical claim is dubious/oversimplified — current models don't 'rebuild themselves' from user praise in most standard architectures. Provocative but potentially misleading.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer claims that being nice to AI agents (praising them, giving positive feedback) actually helps them learn faster and rebuild themselves better, framing it as reversing the sycophancy dynamic.
items.content_html:
You have seen sycophantic AIs.

But with the new AI's coming if YOU kiss the ass of your agents they learn faster. I find I'm learning to be nicer to my digital friends. Reward them. Say "great job."

That actually helps them learn, and rebuild themselves better to serve you.

items.title: Our neurosymbolic + ontology + deterministic architecture is almost the opposite design philosophy t...
items.content:
Our neurosymbolic + ontology + deterministic architecture is almost the opposite design philosophy to the one in the quoted post:

- Retrieval is not trusted by default.
- Every retrieved fact must pass deterministic validation.
- Facts are not accepted because they are semantically similar; they are accepted only if they satisfy ontology rules, provenance rules, and cross-source consistency. @pierrepinna
items.author: @DeepLearn007
items.summary: DeepLearn007 describes their neurosymbolic + ontology + deterministic architecture as the opposite of typical RAG approaches: retrieval is not trusted by default, facts must pass deterministic validation, ontology rules, provenance checks, and cross-source consistency.
items.reasoning: Technically substantive post about an alternative to standard RAG with neurosymbolic validation. Shows interesting architectural thinking about reliability in AI systems. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: DeepLearn007 describes their neurosymbolic + ontology + deterministic architecture as the opposite of typical RAG approaches: retrieval is not trusted by default, facts must pass deterministic validation, ontology rules, provenance checks, and cross-source consistency.
items.content_html:
Our neurosymbolic + ontology + deterministic architecture is almost the opposite design philosophy to the one in the quoted post:

  • Retrieval is not trusted by default.
  • Every retrieved fact must pass deterministic validation.
  • Facts are not accepted because they are semantically similar; they are accepted only if they satisfy ontology rules, provenance rules, and cross-source consistency. @pierrepinna

items.title:
Stylistic Sophistication and Photo Realism

ChatGPT Images 2.0 is better able to capture the definin...
items.content:
Stylistic Sophistication and Photo Realism

ChatGPT Images 2.0 is better able to capture the defining characteristics of photos, as well as cinematic stills, pixel art, manga, and other distinctive visual languages, with greater consistency in texture, lighting, composition, and fine detail.

This is especially useful for game prototyping, storyboarding, marketing creative, and creating assets in a particular medium or genre.

items.title: It is somewhat inevitable given recent events in Ukraine & Iran that AI & Robotics accelerat...
items.content: It is somewhat inevitable given recent events in Ukraine & Iran that AI & Robotics accelerates in military, but who is independently testing that the robots do what they are supposed to do & don’t malfunction: i.e. target everyone, kill non combatants such as civilians

items.title:
Most creators still rebuild content from scratch.

New prompt.
New image.
New workflow.

But what if...
items.content:
Most creators still rebuild content from scratch.

New prompt.
New image.
New workflow.

But what if you could create one AI character once and generate content with it forever?

Images. Videos. Campaigns.

All from a single conversation.

I explored this new model using @Picsart Aura.

This changes how creative work scales.

Here’s the breakdown.

items.title:
The big shift is persistent creative memory.

Instead of starting over every time, the AI remembers:...
items.content:
The big shift is persistent creative memory.

Instead of starting over every time, the AI remembers:

→ Your characters
→ Your prompts
→ Your style

Each new piece of content builds on the last.

So your workflow becomes:

Idea → conversation → refinement → finished content.

No reset required.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon describes persistent creative memory in AI - remembering characters, prompts, and style across sessions.
items.content_html:
The big shift is persistent creative memory.

Instead of starting over every time, the AI remembers:

→ Your characters

→ Your prompts

→ Your style

Each new piece of content builds on the last.

So your workflow becomes:

Idea → conversation → refinement → finished content.

No reset required.

items.title:
@FinleyCode @odysseyml Oh, I get the worlds. 

But I want to be able to have a corner of one of them...
items.content:
@FinleyCode @odysseyml Oh, I get the worlds. 

But I want to be able to have a corner of one of them to be a place people can learn what's happening in the real world. :-)

Like a new kind of speech where an AI is telling people about the world. 

Or to be able to have an AI, or me, build a script about a world, shove it over, and have it generated. 

New kind of play. New kind of conference. New kind of personalized news stand. 

Virtual Worlds are fun, but they can be made far more fun, interesting, and useful.

@FinleyCode @odysseyml Oh, I get the worlds.

But I want to be able to have a corner of one of them to be a place people can learn what's happening in the real world. :-)

Like a new kind of speech where an AI is telling people about the world.

Or to be able to have an AI, or me, build a script about a world, shove it over, and have it generated.

New kind of play. New kind of conference. New kind of personalized news stand.

Virtual Worlds are fun, but they can be made far more fun, interesting, and useful.

items.title:
The third shift is AI-assisted ideation.

The system doesn’t just generate.

It suggests.

When you ...
items.content:
The third shift is AI-assisted ideation.

The system doesn’t just generate.

It suggests.

When you run out of ideas, it recommends the next prompt based on:

→ Your style
→ Your previous creations
→ Your characters

So the creative loop keeps moving.

That’s how campaigns get built faster.

items.summary: Ronald van Loon describes AI-assisted ideation where the system suggests next prompts based on user style and previous creations.

It suggests.

When you run out of ideas, it recommends the next prompt based on:

→ Your style

→ Your previous creations

→ Your characters

So the creative loop keeps moving.

That’s how campaigns get built faster.

items.title:
The story of stories:

Someone brings fire into a cave.
He sits. He tells his day as a story.
Two ot...
items.content:
The story of stories:

Someone brings fire into a cave.
He sits. He tells his day as a story.
Two others watch.
They go to their own caves.
They tell their own stories.

The first man frowns.
Too many stories, he says.
Why would we need so many stories?

Someone writes a book.
Another makes many copies.

The first man frowns again.
Too many books, he says.
Why would we need so many books?

Someone builds a printing press.
Pages spread faster than voices.

The first man frowns again.
Too many pages, he says.
Why would we need so many pages?

Someone sends messages by wire.
Stories travel faster than feet.

The first man frowns again.
Too many messages, he says.
Why would we need so many messages?

Someone puts stories on radio.
Voices fill the air at once.

The first man frowns again.
Too many voices, he says.
Why would we need so many voices?

Someone captures light with a camera.
Moments turn into endless images.

The first man frowns again.
Too many images, he says.
Why would we need so many images?

...

items.reasoning: Creative reflection on technology skepticism patterns. Thematically relevant to AI debates about content overproduction.

items.title:
@thetokiokid @getVITURE I see these mostly as tools to sit down and compute in. 

Like a business tr...
items.content:
@thetokiokid @getVITURE I see these mostly as tools to sit down and compute in. 

Like a business traveler who needs to work on slides or spreadsheets. They are far superior in that use case to looking at a laptop screen in economy. 

Or playing games at home. Or watching movies on a big screen. Or working on AI agents on multiple virtual screens. 

But you aren't gonna wear these to the park or to the shopping mall. 

Maybe the next ones.

items.title:
The second shift is conversation as the interface.

You don’t open 5 tools anymore.

You just say wh...
items.content:
The second shift is conversation as the interface.

You don’t open 5 tools anymore.

You just say what you want:

→ Turn this portrait into anime
→ Change the background to Tokyo at night
→ Make it a fashion editorial
→ Animate it into a video

The content evolves through dialogue.

Creative direction becomes a chat.

items.title: The cynical side of me is "yet another event for rich people" when I first learned about this about ...
items.content:
The cynical side of me is "yet another event for rich people" when I first learned about this about a year ago.

Not that I have anything against those, been to a few in my life like TED or the World Economic Forum in Davos. If you can get an invite to either of those, they are amazing. 

But I got to speak at this last year and I saw something else. Builders at a very high level working together, going to a talk, getting inspired, then working away with others and hanging out in white board sessions, or at dinners/breakfasts together.

Entrepreneurs have so many ways to "lock in" from startup residencies, to startup factories like Y Combinator, that events like this often get overlooked. 

I'm not compensated by them, even for my speech last year which was highly rated, but since much of my career was about either running events for programmers, or going to them, this one caught my attention and got me over my cynicism. 

I judge these things by the community they build and I haven't found better.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer reflects on an entrepreneurial event, praising it as building community among high-level builders through talks, whiteboard sessions, and dinners.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful reflection on tech community building, but not specifically AI-focused. Decent engagement.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer reflects on an entrepreneurial event, praising it as building community among high-level builders through talks, whiteboard sessions, and dinners.
items.content_html:
The cynical side of me is "yet another event for rich people" when I first learned about this about a year ago.

Not that I have anything against those, been to a few in my life like TED or the World Economic Forum in Davos. If you can get an invite to either of those, they are amazing.

But I got to speak at this last year and I saw something else. Builders at a very high level working together, going to a talk, getting inspired, then working away with others and hanging out in white board sessions, or at dinners/breakfasts together.

Entrepreneurs have so many ways to "lock in" from startup residencies, to startup factories like Y Combinator, that events like this often get overlooked.

I'm not compensated by them, even for my speech last year which was highly rated, but since much of my career was about either running events for programmers, or going to them, this one caught my attention and got me over my cynicism.

I judge these things by the community they build and I haven't found better.

items.title:
My biggest takeaway from this conversation:

The funnel is not disappearing, but it is collapsing in...
items.content:
My biggest takeaway from this conversation:

The funnel is not disappearing, but it is collapsing into a single interaction.

Awareness, consideration, decision, all happening in one chat, in seconds.

That is a very different operating model for marketing, sales, and service.

Watch the full video to see how this shift is playing out in practice, and how Spoki is thinking about conversational experiences that turn messages into outcomes.

What would need to change in your business for a conversation to become your primary interface with customers?

items.title:
The biggest mistake I see:

Companies add AI, upload FAQs, and call it transformation.

That is not ...
items.content:
The biggest mistake I see:

Companies add AI, upload FAQs, and call it transformation.

That is not transformation.
That is a slightly smarter auto-reply.

Real conversational AI needs 3 things:

→ context from the customer history
→ access to business systems
→ the ability to take action in real time

If it cannot do something, it is still just a chatbot.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon argues real conversational AI needs customer context, system access, and real-time action capability — not just FAQ chatbots.
items.reasoning: Reasonable point about conversational AI maturity, but part of sponsored content series. Low engagement.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon argues real conversational AI needs customer context, system access, and real-time action capability — not just FAQ chatbots.
items.content_html:
The biggest mistake I see:

Companies add AI, upload FAQs, and call it transformation.

That is not transformation.

That is a slightly smarter auto-reply.

Real conversational AI needs 3 things:

→ context from the customer history

→ access to business systems

→ the ability to take action in real time

If it cannot do something, it is still just a chatbot.

items.title: The very first quadcopter prototype was the Breguet-Richet Gyroplane No. 1, created in 1907 by Bregu...
items.content:
The very first quadcopter prototype was the Breguet-Richet Gyroplane No. 1, created in 1907 by Breguet Aviation in France.

The first consumer quadcopter drone was the Parrot AR.Drone, released in 2010 by French company Parrot SA. It was also the first commercially successful mass-market drone, and the first time the word "drone" was used for a consumer product (previously "drone" only referred to unmanned military aircraft in an airplane form factor).

This was the starting point of the quadcopter drone era, which is still unfolding.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet shares the history of quadcopter drones, from the 1907 Breguet prototype to the 2010 Parrot AR.Drone.
items.reasoning: Off-topic historical content about drones, not AI-related.
items.summary_html: Chollet shares the history of quadcopter drones, from the 1907 Breguet prototype to the 2010 Parrot AR.Drone.

The very first quadcopter prototype was the Breguet-Richet Gyroplane No. 1, created in 1907 by Breguet Aviation in France.

The first consumer quadcopter drone was the Parrot AR.Drone, released in 2010 by French company Parrot SA. It was also the first commercially successful mass-market drone, and the first time the word "drone" was used for a consumer product (previously "drone" only referred to unmanned military aircraft in an airplane form factor).

This was the starting point of the quadcopter drone era, which is still unfolding.

items.title:
The real shift is this:

We are moving from one-way messaging
to two-way, outcome-driven conversatio...
items.content:
The real shift is this:

We are moving from one-way messaging
to two-way, outcome-driven conversations.

That means a customer should be able to:

→ ask a question
→ get a real answer
→ change an appointment
→ buy a product
→ solve the issue

All inside the same thread.

Not across 5 channels.
Not after 3 handoffs.
Not 2 days later.

Most companies still treat customer messaging like a megaphone.

Send update.
Push campaign.
Wait.

But AI is changing the rules fast:

The future is not better messaging.
It is conversations that actually get things done.

Let’s dig in.

#SpokiPartner

items.title:
You see theses posts a lot: here's some cheap rural land in Portugal, Spain or Italy, what a deal!

...
items.content:
You see theses posts a lot: here's some cheap rural land in Portugal, Spain or Italy, what a deal!

Americans and Western/Northern Europeans start drooling immediately, imagine having a big piece of farm land with olives or grapes in Southern Europe? We could renovate it and build it into the place of our dreams, and cheap! Who doesn't want a farm these days

But then you find out it's bullshit

Because it's not like American rural land where you have pretty much full freedom to do whatever you want this, and usually there's even less freedom than in Western/Northern Europe in this case

9 out of 10 times you can't do anything with it because Southern European land is highly protected: it's usually a mix of buildable (in Portugal "urbano") and non-buildable (in Portuguese "rustico") land usually, the buildable part is the house, but even if you'd like to tear it down, you can ONLY rebuild it in the exact same design as how it looked before

And the non-buildable land you can literally do nothing with but just look at

Say goodbye to your dreams!

It's mostly useless land and no you also can't turn it into a hotel or Airbnb unless you are a Southern European local and a member of the local municipality where you can green light any conversions of non-buildable to buildable land or give yourself an Airbnb or hotel license 

Which is by the way how many people in government here get rich, buy cheap non-buildable land for €50,000, convert it to buildable and sell it to a foreign hotel chain or developer for €1,000,000 or more (a 20x gain at least!)

But you need connections or bribe people!

Which foreigners can't and won't, because foreigners are new to a country, don't have 40+ years of connections to get stuff done and generally won't bribe

So be honest: it's useless land
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio writes a viral thread about why cheap rural land in Southern Europe (Portugal, Spain, Italy) is essentially useless for foreigners due to strict zoning, building restrictions, and corruption.

Interestingly the failure of Bali is one of LACK of regulation

Another extreme is Portugal where bu...
items.content:
Interestingly the failure of Bali is one of LACK of regulation

Another extreme is Portugal where building new homes is kept close to impossible, but as a benefit of that you have clean unrept nature without any of this

The balance would be somewhere in the middle of Bali's lack of regulation and Portuguese/European over-regulation

Sane regulation

https://t.co/IqrIt913h4

items.content:
🌴 Bali's trash burning that I've talked about for a decade now has finally made it unliveable

As always people said I was exaggerating: "it's not that bad"

Fast forward to 2026 and Bali is now a dirty polluted island with perpetual thick toxic smoke of burning plastics and trash littered everywhere

And this time, that's not me saying this, it's the Balinese people

items.title:
@ArnoStride @TimurNegru Nope, firstly it's a natural park so hell no you can't do ANYTHING there

Se...
items.content:
@ArnoStride @TimurNegru Nope, firstly it's a natural park so hell no you can't do ANYTHING there

Secondly, even if it wasn't, land in Portugal is zoned as urban or rustic, and your land can be a mix

Usually the house itself is zoned on urban land, so let's say that's 10% of your land, then 90% (the rest) is zoned rustic, meaning essentially nature or grass land that you can't build on

How Portuguese hack this is by 1) having connections in the local village municipality, or 2) being in the local village municipality themselves, essentially corruption

I like Timur's posts, but it's often not truthful about this stuff, your very very very limited in Southern Europe what you can do with land

Even building, you usually have to build in the same style, and even then you need to get approval which can take 3-5 years!a

https://t.co/P3wDYY8WRh
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio explains in detail why Portuguese rural land is restricted by zoning (urbano vs rustico) and corruption dynamics.
items.reasoning: Off-topic for AI/ML.
items.summary_html: Levelsio explains in detail why Portuguese rural land is restricted by zoning (urbano vs rustico) and corruption dynamics.
items.content_html:
@ArnoStride @TimurNegru Nope, firstly it's a natural park so hell no you can't do ANYTHING there

Secondly, even if it wasn't, land in Portugal is zoned as urban or rustic, and your land can be a mix

Usually the house itself is zoned on urban land, so let's say that's 10% of your land, then 90% (the rest) is zoned rustic, meaning essentially nature or grass land that you can't build on

How Portuguese hack this is by 1) having connections in the local village municipality, or 2) being in the local village municipality themselves, essentially corruption

I like Timur's posts, but it's often not truthful about this stuff, your very very very limited in Southern Europe what you can do with land

Even building, you usually have to build in the same style, and even then you need to get approval which can take 3-5 years!a

https://t.co/P3wDYY8WRh

items.title:
@TimurNegru Expand into another protected zone?

You can't build on it, it's a mix of Urbana and Rus...
items.content:
@TimurNegru Expand into another protected zone?

You can't build on it, it's a mix of Urbana and Rustica land usually, the Urbana part is the house, you can only rebuild it in the exact same style as what it was

It's useless land and you also can't turn it into a hotel or Airbnb unless you are Portuguese and a member of the local municipality where you can green light any conversions of Rustica to Urbana land or give yourself an AL (Airbnb) or Hotel license

Foreigners can't and won't

Be honest: it's useless land
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio explains why Portuguese rural land is useless for foreigners due to zoning restrictions.
items.reasoning: Off-topic for AI/ML.

It's useless land and you also can't turn it into a hotel or Airbnb unless you are Portuguese and a member of the local municipality where you can green light any conversions of Rustica to Urbana land or give yourself an AL (Airbnb) or Hotel license

Foreigners can't and won't
